MedNet-XS: An Efficient Deep Network for Chest X-ray Classification

## 1 Introduction

Chest X-ray (CXR) classification is a fundamental task in computer-aided diagnosis. Existing models like DenseNet-121 and EfficientNet-B0 achieve high accuracy but are too large for edge deployment in primary care clinics. We present MedNet-XS, a compact CNN tailored for CXR classification, optimized for both accuracy and parameter efficiency. Our design goal is to match DenseNet-121 accuracy within 1.5M parameters, making it suitable for mobile and embedded devices.

## 2 Related Work

CXR classification has been advanced by deep learning on large public datasets. CheXpert (Stanford, 224k images, 14 labels) and ChestX-ray14 (NIH, 112k images, 14 labels) are the two most widely used benchmarks. Prior work focuses on transfer learning from ImageNet, label smoothing for noisy radiology labels, and class-imbalanced loss functions (focal loss, class-balanced loss). However, model compression for CXR has been less explored.

## 3 Method

MedNet-XS is a 1.4M-parameter network with three design principles. First, depthwise separable convolutions replace standard convolutions in the middle layers, reducing FLOPs by 4x. Second, we use a Squeeze-and-Excitation channel attention module before each downsampling step, recalibrating feature importance. Third, we adopt a multi-label binary cross-entropy loss with positive-negative balancing, since each image can have multiple co-occurring pathologies.

The architecture has four stages with channel widths 16, 32, 64, 128, downsampling by stride-2 convolutions. Global average pooling is followed by a fully connected classifier with 14 sigmoid outputs.

## 4 Experiment

We evaluate MedNet-XS on the CheXpert dataset using the official train/val split (223k/234 images). We report macro-averaged AUC across 5 representative pathologies: atelectasis, cardiomegaly, consolidation, edema, and pleural effusion. Following the CheXpert protocol, uncertain labels are mapped to positive (U-Ones strategy). All models are trained for 6 epochs using AdamW optimizer with learning rate 1e-3, batch size 256, on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

Baselines include DenseNet-121 (8.1M params), EfficientNet-B0 (5.3M params), and ResNet-18 (11.7M params). All baselines are trained with the same protocol for fair comparison.

## 5 Result

MedNet-XS achieves a macro-AUC of 0.857 on CheXpert, surpassing DenseNet-121 (0.853) while using 5.8x fewer parameters. On the NIH ChestX-ray14 benchmark, MedNet-XS reaches 0.831 macro-AUC, comparable to EfficientNet-B0 (0.834). The model's small footprint (1.4M params, 5.6MB checkpoint) makes it deployable on mobile devices, with inference latency under 30ms on a single CPU thread.

Ablation on CheXpert shows that depthwise separable convolutions reduce parameters by 65% with only 0.4% accuracy loss. SE channel attention adds 0.7% AUC. The combined design achieves the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

## 6 Conclusion

MedNet-XS demonstrates that carefully designed compact CNNs can match or exceed larger baselines on CXR classification. The model is suitable for on-device deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings. Future work includes extending the architecture to multi-view CXR and exploring knowledge distillation from larger teacher networks.
